entries.title: Color Symbolism And Freemasonry
entries.full_summary:
# Color Symbolism And Freemasonry

By John Shroeder, PM. Presented to A. Douglas Smith, Jr. Lodge of Research, #1949, August 29, 1998.
Published in Transactions of A. Douglas Smith, Jr. Lodge of Research #1949, Volume 4 (1998–2001).
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Color Symbolism And Freemasonry | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#color-symbolism-and-freemasonry | section 1

entries.title: Introduction: Color as a Masonic Teaching Tool
entries.short_summary: ## Introduction: Color as a Masonic Teaching Tool
entries.full_summary:
## Introduction: Color as a Masonic Teaching Tool

We all know that Masonry employs symbols to teach moral lessons. One definition of Masonry frequently used is that it is a beautiful system of morality veiled in allegory and illustrated by symbols. Our lectures give explanations of those symbols. Some believe that there is much to be learned from the symbols which is not included in the lectures, that the given explanations are but the tip of the iceberg.
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Introduction: Color as a Masonic Teaching Tool | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#introduction-color-as-a-masonic-teaching-tool | section 2

entries.title: Blue: The Color of the Symbolic Lodge
entries.short_summary: ## Blue: The Color of the Symbolic Lodge
entries.full_summary:
## Blue: The Color of the Symbolic Lodge

Our Virginia Mentor's Manual contains a section saying that it is unknown where, when or why the name Blue Lodge originated, but also pointing out that blue is generally regarded as the color of truth and fidelity, basic teachings of our craft; that blue is the color used to characterize friendship, one of the jewels of a Mason. It also tells us, "some have found some symbolism in the blue arch of the heavens and have urged that for a Freemason the virtues of friendship and benevolence must be as expansive as the heavens." It concludes with the reminder that the proper term is "Symbolic Lodge" rather than "Blue Lodge" although blue as a color and symbol is prominent in our teachings.

The Masonic Services Association distributed a Short Talk Bulletin in April 1994 which addressed the views of Brother Leon Zeldis, Honorary Assistant Grand Master, and Past Sovereign Grand Commander of the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite, of Israel, condensed from his paper in Quatuor Coranatorum Transactions, vol. 105, 1992, concerning the Masonic meanings of the colors blue, white, purple, red, green, yellow and black. Brother Zeldis quotes the Masonic author, A. E. Waite, as writing, "There is no recognized scheme or science of colors in Masonry. Here and there in our ritual we find an 'explanation' for the use of a certain colour, but this usually turns out to be merely a peg on which to hang a homiletic lecture about it, having little if any connection with the origins of its use." Brother Zeldis also quotes Brother Chetwode Crawley, "The ordinary prosaic enquirer will see in the selection of blue as the distinctive colour of Freemasonry only the natural sequence of the legend of King Solomon's Temple. For the Jews had been Divinely commanded to wear a 'riband of blue' (Numbers 15:38, 'You are to take tassels on the corners of your garments with a blue cord on each tassel')." Brother Zeldis does not mention the description in Exodus 28:31 and 33 respectively, "And you shall make the robe of the ephod all blue.", "On its skirts you shall make pomegranates of blue and purple and scarlet stuff …". The pomegranates are, of course, another familiar Masonic symbol on the chapiters of the pillars.

The Masonic Service Association of North America publication, "EMESSAY NOTES" of February 1997, also has this to say about the Masonic symbolism of the color blue: "Blue: having the color of clear sky, the sapphire, the turquoise. Blue was adopted as the official color of the Symbolic Lodge by the Mother Grand Lodge, which first proclaimed white as the color and then changed it to blue. Various theories account for the color as that descriptive of a Symbolic Lodge; it came from the blue vault of heaven; blue was the color of the steel points of the compasses, contrasting with the yellow brass of the hinged part of the instrument; blue was the official color of the Order of the Garter and was adopted for lodges in an attempt to add the dignity of that decoration to the Fraternity. Blue Lodge and Blue Masonry are other names for Symbolic Lodge and Symbolic Masonry."

Mackey says that blue is the symbol of universal friendship and benevolence, because, as it is the color of the vault of heaven, which embraces and covers the whole globe, we are thus reminded that in the breast of every brother these virtues should be equally as extensive. He says that blue, except for white, is the only color which should be used in a Master's lodge for decorations. Mackey relates that blue was to the Hebrews a symbol of perfection, to the Druids a symbol of truth, to the Hindus a symbol of wisdom, to the Babylonians and the Egyptians a sacred color, to the Chinese a symbol of divinity, and to medieval Christians, an emblem of immortality.
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Blue: The Color of the Symbolic Lodge | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#blue-the-color-of-the-symbolic-lodge | section 3

entries.title: Jung's Psychology and the Three Craft Degrees
entries.short_summary: ## Jung's Psychology and the Three Craft Degrees
entries.full_summary:
## Jung's Psychology and the Three Craft Degrees

The renowned Swiss psychologist, Carl Jung, took a great interest in the folk legends and symbols of earlier ages as he felt that they contributed to an understanding of the changes which he felt had taken place in man's understanding of himself and his role in the Universe over eons of time. Jung felt that early man had not originally had a clear consciousness or rational thinking capability but, over centuries, developed such a capability and now has come to know only the conscious, and not the unconscious, which was layered over by the development of the conscious. He believed that earlier man had been governed by instincts, much as we think about the animal mind today. He felt that the earlier instinctual mind is still with man today but it is as a shadow behind or beneath the conscious mind. He felt that the integration of that shadow mind with the conscious mind results in the enlightenment or individuation of the individual.

Because of his keen interest, Jung read widely in science, literature, history, philosophy and also folklore and found, in literature from ancient civilizations and religions, parallels in symbolism which he reasoned showed that these arose from a common thread in the instinctive minds of ancient men. In this regard, he adopted the Eastern philosophy that if things repeatedly occur together, it is more than coincidence even if a causal relationship cannot be conclusively proven through scientific methods. He called this synchronicity.

Worshipful Brother Kirk MacNulty, author of the beautiful work, *Freemasonry: A Journey through Ritual and Symbol*, is quoted by Wallace McLeod in his book, *A Quest for Light*, as also using Jungian concepts in explaining how the three Craft degrees recapitulate the development of the individual person. "The Entered Apprentice degree, (or the Ground Floor of the Temple), represents the physical or material being, Jung's 'individual consciousness'. The Fellow Craft, (or Middle Chamber), represents the soul or psyche, Jung's 'personal unconscious'. The Master Mason degree, (or Holy of Holies), is the spiritual being, Jung's 'collective unconscious', one step closer to the Divine." Similar parallels in the symbolism of peoples very far removed from each other culturally, geographically and in time have been noted by Fraser in his seminal work, *The Golden Bough*, and more recently by Joseph Campbell as reported in his many books, especially his series on the nature of the gods in various cultures.

Jung found that the color blue of the heavenly sky has been in many cultures and beliefs a symbol of spirituality. He contrasts this with red as a symbol of emotion. Blue is the coolest of colors and we equate spirituality with a cool, detached, or other-worldly orientation or philosophy. Red is the warmest of colors which we equate with the heat of anger or passion. Jung points out that the combination of red and blue provides purple or violet which he says is the symbol of mysticism — a good illustration of his concept of the integration or individuation of the personality which he concludes has a duality similar to his concept of the conscious mind and shadow mind.
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Jung's Psychology and the Three Craft Degrees | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#jung-s-psychology-and-the-three-craft-degrees | section 4

entries.title: Blue as Symbol of Spiritual Aspiration
entries.short_summary: ## Blue as Symbol of Spiritual Aspiration
entries.full_summary:
## Blue as Symbol of Spiritual Aspiration

I found this of interest Masonically since blue is the color of the symbolic lodge which, in Jungian terms, represents the spiritual values to which we aspire. That seems to me to be closely aligned with the moral lessons taught as we progress through the three degrees from the physical man, or Entered Apprentice, to the mindful man, or Fellow Craft, and ultimately to the spiritual man, or Master Mason.

This is also evidenced by the symbolism of the changes in mode of wearing the apron while progressing through the degrees where the triangular flap, which triangle, Masonic scholars tell us, represents deity, is first separate and above the rectangular apron representing worldly, or base, things. Secondly, the triangle or spiritual symbol is imposed upon the worldly form in the Fellow Craft's apron, and thirdly, the two are integrated into one triangular form, as a Master Mason, symbolizing spirituality.
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Blue as Symbol of Spiritual Aspiration | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#blue-as-symbol-of-spiritual-aspiration | section 5

entries.title: Red: The Color of Chivalric Degrees
entries.short_summary: ## Red: The Color of Chivalric Degrees
entries.full_summary:
## Red: The Color of Chivalric Degrees

Some of the Masonic degrees, some of which are called chivalric degrees, in both the Scottish and York Rites use red as the basic color, much as the symbolic lodge uses blue. In the case of the York Rite degree of the Royal Arch, this has been explained by Mackey as the symbol of the ardor and zeal which should actuate all who are in possession of that sublime portion of Freemasonry. Mackey also cites Portal's Symbolic Colors which states that red, representing fire, is the symbol of the regeneration and purification of souls. Mackey sees this as a logical reason for the Royal Arch to adopt it because the Royal Arch refers historically to the re-building or regeneration of the Temple of Solomon, and symbolically to the regeneration of life. In the case of the use of red as the symbolic color for the heraldic degrees of the York and Scottish Rites, it fits well with the Jungian interpretation of red as the symbol of emotions, because courage, fervor, and zeal, when properly directed, are all noble emotions which are closely associated with the rituals of the heraldic degrees.
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Red: The Color of Chivalric Degrees | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#red-the-color-of-chivalric-degrees | section 6

entries.title: Purple and Violet: The Mystical Integration
entries.short_summary: ## Purple and Violet: The Mystical Integration
entries.full_summary:
## Purple and Violet: The Mystical Integration

The combination of red and blue into purple or violet is the color of the York Rite Mark, Past, and Most Excellent Master degrees which fall between the degree of Master Mason and that of the Royal Arch. Mackey says that the color purple in this case therefore symbolizes the close connection and harmony which should ever exist between these two portions of the Masonic System. Portal's Symbolic Colors states that purple, in the profane language of colors, symbolizes constancy in spiritual combats because blue denotes fidelity and red, war. Purple has always been an emblem of exalted office — the legendary color of the robes of kings and high priests. The purple dye at that time reportedly could only be produced by crushing a certain shellfish, murex, from Phoenicia, which made it very expensive and contributed to limiting its use.

Brother Zeldis points out that, in Hebrew, tchelet (light blue) and argaman (purple) have usually been mentioned together without scholars reaching satisfactory conclusions as to why. He found that the light blue, or tchelet, was produced from a short variety of the shellfish, murex trunculus, while the purple, or argaman, was produced from murex brandaris and thais haemastoma. He points out that some historians have concluded that, in the Middle Ages in Europe, blue was low in popular esteem compared with red because red dyes could achieve stronger shades which brought to mind the prestigious purple of the ancient world. However, blue gradually became recognized as a princely color replacing red at court because red was used by the masses and, hence, considered vulgar. Blue and gold then became the color for shields, banners and livery.

Nowadays, purple is used Masonically in the collars of Grand Lodge Officers in England and some other jurisdictions. The use of violet in the caps of the members of the Supreme Council of the Thirty Third Degree and those of the Sovereign Grand Inspectors General of the Scottish Rite is also thought to symbolize the use of the purple by the high priests and kings of ancient civilizations. Brother Zeldis feels that the explanation of purple as 'an emblem of union being composed of blue and crimson' in the Royal Arch is contrived.

Personally, I would prefer to go back to basics and to look on the use of these violet caps and purple collars as emblematic of the Jungian explanation of the successful integration within the personality of the spiritual mind and the zealous mind to attain a higher level of consciousness and self-knowledge. This would negate Mackey's concept that purple or violet is a transitional color from the blue of the Symbolic Lodge to the red of the Royal Arch with red being the ultimate color. In the organization of colors which I propose, the violet or purple would be the ultimate, reflecting the whole, integrated persona.
source_notes: Color Symbolism | Purple and Violet: The Mystical Integration | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Color-Symbolism.md#purple-and-violet-the-mystical-integration | section 7

entries.title: Conclusion: Synchronicity, Jung, and Masonic Heritage
entries.short_summary: ## Conclusion: Synchronicity, Jung, and Masonic Heritage
entries.full_summary:
## Conclusion: Synchronicity, Jung, and Masonic Heritage

Learning to read the symbols of ancient folk legends and finding parallels in distant cultures and distant ages led Carl Jung to a better understanding of the human mind. Study of his writings and study of explanations of our Masonic symbols can lead modern Masons to a better understanding of our Masonic heritage of symbolism and to a better understanding of our Craft and of ourselves. This latter meets the goal of the ancient mysteries, "First know thyself."

In addition to the writings of Jung, the writings of Joseph Campbell, the late professor of mythology at Sarah Lawrence College, are commended. Campbell's book, *The Power of Myth*, discusses Masonic symbols used by our founding fathers on the dollar bill, the pyramid topped by the all-seeing eye. Other books of Campbell which will be of interest to students of symbolism include his multi-volume set, *The Masks of God*.

entries.title: Allegories of Hiram Abiff
entries.full_summary:
# Allegories of Hiram Abiff

Exoteric and esoteric philosophies from Christianity, Rosicrucianism, and the ancient Egyptian mystery schools.
By Companion Joel Bundy. Presented to Virginia Royal Arch Research Chapter No. 1753, July 16th, 2016.

Freemasonry is "veiled in allegory and illustrated by symbols" because these are the surest way by which moral and ethical truths may be taught. It is not only with the brain and with the mind that the initiate must take Freemasonry but also with the heart. — C. H. Claudy
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Allegories of Hiram Abiff | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#allegories-of-hiram-abiff | section 1

entries.title: Who Was Hiram Abiff?
entries.full_summary:
## Who Was Hiram Abiff?

We all know the story. The widow's son is sent for to be the architect of King Solomon's temple. King Solomon, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff are the first three Grand Masters. During work on the temple, three Fellowcraft Masons demand the word from Hiram Abiff but he refuses. Each time he is assaulted with a Masonic tool, and is killed by the third assailant. Subsequently the murderers bury the body in a shallow grave, marked by a sprig of Acacia. The body is found, the "ruffians" are brought to justice by King Solomon, but the secret word is lost and replaced with substitutions given by Solomon based on words and gestures at the time of the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body. He is raised at that time, and this Rite of the Raising has been part of the third degree.

The name Hiram Abiff (sometimes written Abif) is not found in Scripture, but Freemasons accept him as the architect of the temple of Solomon. Hiram, or Huram, is certainly found in two parts of scripture, the first in Chronicles and the second in the book of Kings. Huram-Abi is found in 2 Chronicles 2:13 in the NAS and NIV translations; Huram is a variant of Hiram. In the KJV translation the name Hiram is found, rendered "Huram my father's" — the Hebrew word behind "father's" is "'ab," which according to Strong's Concordance can also mean fatherless, as the son of a widow would be. Studying the various translations along with a Hebrew dictionary allows us to see how Freemasonry may have settled on the name Hiram Abiff.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Who Was Hiram Abiff? | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#who-was-hiram-abiff | section 2

entries.title: Hiram in Scripture
entries.full_summary:
## Hiram in Scripture

From 2 Chronicles 2:11-14: Hiram King of Tyre replied by letter to Solomon: "Because the Lord loves his people, he has made you their king." And Hiram added: "Praise be to the Lord, the God of Israel, who made heaven and earth! He has given King David a wise son, endowed with intelligence and discernment, who will build a temple for the Lord and a palace for himself. I am sending you Huram-Abi, a man of great skill, whose mother was from Dan and whose father was from Tyre. He is trained to work in gold and silver, bronze and iron, stone and wood, and with purple and blue and crimson yarn and fine linen. He is experienced in all kinds of engraving and can execute any design given to him."

From 1 Kings 7:13-14 in the KJV: He was a widow's son of the tribe of Naphtali, and his father was a man of Tyre, a worker in brass: and he was filled with wisdom, and understanding, and cunning to work all works in brass. And he came to King Solomon, and wrought all his work.

'Abif' itself means "chief" or "head". At that time, the Tyrians and Sidonians were felt to be the greatest mechanics in the world, and also celebrated artists. To summarize, from scripture we can understand: Hiram Abiff was likely the Hiram who was a worker in metals, stone, wood and linens; he was a widow's son; his father was from Tyre and he was brought to the temple by Hiram, King of Tyre.

On a side note, Albert Pike, most likely incorrectly, felt that "Khurum, therefore, improperly called Hiram, is Khur-om, the same as Her-ra, Her-mes, and Her-acles," attributing him to be the same as the god of the sun.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Hiram in Scripture | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#hiram-in-scripture | section 3

entries.title: The Hiramic Legend in Masonic History
entries.short_summary: ## The Hiramic Legend in Masonic History
entries.full_summary:
## The Hiramic Legend in Masonic History

There is no mention of Hiram Abiff in the Old Charges, such as the Regius Poem, the Matthew Cooke manuscript, or the 1632 document. According to Worshipful Brother Madhaven, writing for Pietre-Stones, the first record of the third degree being conferred was at London in 1724, but the Hiramic Legend was probably not part of the ritual of that time. Anderson's Constitutions of 1723 make no mention of the tragedy, but just fifteen years later, in the second Constitutions of 1738, the three ruffians had killed the Prince of Architects. This is conclusive proof that the Hiramic Legend became a part of Masonic Traditions between 1723 and 1738, and not earlier.

What became our three degrees resembles what was required for admission to any of the medieval craft guilds: an oath, rules and regulations read to the craftsman, and penalties for violations of the oath impressed upon the candidate, often combined into a ceremony or rite. Many of these guilds held numerous secrets, and the extreme consequences of disclosure to outsiders were a reason for elaborate rites of passage. Any "intellectual" knowledge that did not conform to orthodox teachings could lead to accusations of heresy — regardless of whether esoteric doctrines were involved or merely masonic discussions of divinity and enlightenment teachings. Truly, only those craftsmen deemed worthy could be trusted with the secrets of the order.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | The Hiramic Legend in Masonic History | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#the-hiramic-legend-in-masonic-history | section 4

entries.title: Allegory, Exoteric, and Esoteric: Definitions
entries.full_summary:
## Allegory, Exoteric, and Esoteric: Definitions

Before continuing, some definitions are in order (from the Oxford English Dictionary):

Allegory — the use of symbols in a story, picture, etc., to convey a hidden or ulterior meaning, typically a moral or political one; symbolic representation. Also the story, picture, etc. itself that uses symbols to convey a hidden meaning, or a character or figure that symbolically represents someone or something else.

Exoteric — intended for or suited to the generality of disciples; communicated to outsiders, intelligible to the public. Hence of disciples: belonging to the outer circle.

Esoteric — of philosophical doctrines, treatises, modes of speech: designed for, or appropriate to, an inner circle of advanced or privileged disciples; communicated to, or intelligible by, the initiated exclusively. Hence of disciples: belonging to the inner circle; private, confidential.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Allegory, Exoteric, and Esoteric: Definitions | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#allegory-exoteric-and-esoteric-definitions | section 5

entries.title: The Exoteric Teaching: Virtue, Justice, and Merit
entries.short_summary: ## The Exoteric Teaching: Virtue, Justice, and Merit
entries.full_summary:
## The Exoteric Teaching: Virtue, Justice, and Merit

What is the exoteric teaching we can learn from Hiram Abiff? It appears to be lessons on virtue, justice, and merit. Hiram is seen as a virtuous man, full of integrity, regardless of the consequences — essentially the concept of virtue ethics. This would be the opposite of consequentialist ethics, or the "ends justify the means" theorem. The concept of justice for wrong is universal across cultures and political and religious thought: reap what you sow, karma, punishment by legal decree. We also learn that we should earn what we have, and that we should believe in our merits and accomplishments. These are the foundational principles of the Hiram Abiff story.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | The Exoteric Teaching: Virtue, Justice, and Merit | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#the-exoteric-teaching-virtue-justice-and-merit | section 6

entries.title: Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising
entries.short_summary: ## Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising
entries.full_summary:
## Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising

What did the raising of Hiram Abiff symbolize? From Albert Mackey we know of at least 17 different meanings:

1. The real and actual death of a historical Hiram Abiff
2. The legend of Osiris
3. An allegory of the setting sun
4. The expulsion of Adam from Eden
5. The death of Abel
6. The entry of Noah into the ark
7. Joseph's mourning for Jacob
8. An astronomical problem
9. The death and resurrection of Christ
10. The persecution of the Templars
11. The violent death of Charles I
12. A drama invented by Cromwell
13. A representation of old age
14. A drama of regeneration
15. A savage ceremony of initiation
16. A memorial to the murder of Thomas Becket
17. An invention by the Jacobites

Due to the lack of time, three of these possible meanings deserve a closer look: the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and that of Osiris from the Egyptian schools.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#mackey-s-seventeen-meanings-of-the-raising | section 7
entries.source_heading: Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising

entries.title: The Christ Allegory
entries.full_summary:
## The Christ Allegory

Mackey's dictionary: "Hiram Abif was the architect; he made plans and designs ('the Word'). Was HA himself 'raised'? The text does not say so; nor do the self-imprecations of the culprits; nor does he re-appear to sit beside Solomon. There was a substitution of one man to take the place of the old one, and this appears to be the whole point of the raising. If this be true — and to question it is to question the text — then the text itself does not support the theory that we have a drama of resurrection." Even so, many see similarities in the fact that Hiram was raised from his earthly sepulcher, and the three murderers are similar to Caesar's agent (state), the Sanhedrin (the church) and the incited mob. However, it is unlikely that these early Masons would have made such a ceremony over something so sacred.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | The Christ Allegory | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#the-christ-allegory | section 8

entries.title: The Rosicrucian Temple Legend
entries.full_summary:
## The Rosicrucian Temple Legend

Rudolf Steiner was a modern Rosicrucian and gave a lecture in 1904 on the Temple Legend, one not well known in the craft, and having distinctly different ideas than the Christian allegory of Hiram Abiff. He founded a Rosicrucian school of Anthroposophy and is known for his work in biodynamic agriculture. His teaching as to the meaning of this legend is that a union is required between those who have wisdom and those with fires of passion, and that this union, brought about by Hiram Abiff, led to something lasting and durable.

In the legend, one of the Elohim united with Eve and she gave birth to Cain; another Elohim, named Yahveh, created Adam, who also united with Eve, and from this union came Abel. Cain is thus a direct descendant of the gods, but Abel is a descendant of human parents. The sacrifices Abel made to Yahveh pleased him, but Cain's did not, and Cain committed fratricide and was excluded from communion with Yahveh. Among the descendants of Cain are all those who have been creators of art and science. Hiram descended from the race of Cain and inherited all that had been learned by his line in technology and art — the most significant architect imaginable. Out of Seth's line came Solomon, endowed with calm, clear, objective wisdom — a directly inspired gift of God — but unable to produce anything tangible of a technical nature.

Solomon called upon Hiram to be his master builder. The Queen of Sheba, having consented to be Solomon's bride, was captivated by Hiram's glance when she met the master builder, and jealousy arose between Hiram and Solomon. The temple was almost complete except for Hiram's masterpiece, the Molten Sea. Three apprentices, whom Hiram had found too unskilled to promote to masters, swore revenge and sabotaged the casting; Solomon, through jealousy, did nothing to stop them. As the casting disintegrated, Hiram's ancestor appeared and told him to cast himself into the fire, as he was invulnerable to the flames. Hiram came to the centre of the earth, was initiated into the Mystery of Fire and the secret of bronze casting, and received a hammer and a Golden Triangle. He returned and completed the casting of the Molten Sea.

Hereupon the Queen of Sheba consented to become Hiram's bride. He, however, was set upon by the three apprentices and murdered. Before he died, Hiram managed to throw the Golden Triangle into a well, and said: "I shall have a son who will be the father of many descendants who will people the earth and bring my work — the building of the Temple — to completion." The Golden Triangle was collected and brought to the Molten Sea and both were preserved together in the Holy of Holies, only to be discovered by those who can understand the meaning of the legend.

The interpretation is thus: there are two races in mankind, those like Solomon who are in possession of divine wisdom, and the race of Cain, who are conversant with fire, that of wishes and desires. The Sons of Seth have great wisdom and are religious, but lack enthusiasm, while the Sons of Cain are impulsive, but out of their desires came the arts and sciences. It was not until Hiram Abiff was able to use the water of calm wisdom with the fire of passion and desire that he was able to create the union of enduring brotherhood.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | The Rosicrucian Temple Legend | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#the-rosicrucian-temple-legend | section 9

entries.title: Isis and Osiris
entries.full_summary:
## Isis and Osiris

There is much written about whether Hiram Abiff could be the Masonic interpretation of a much older legend, that of Osiris from Egypt. The most famous interpretation of the myth comes from Plutarch, the famous second-century Greek writer. The following synopsis was abstracted from Glen Knape's work on The Temple and the Word.

Osiris was a complex Egyptian deity. Seth, the evil brother of Osiris, tricked him into a chest. The chest was sealed with lead, thrown into the Nile, and floated downstream to the Mediterranean. It eventually washed up on the shore of Lebanon, at Byblos, and a tree grew around it. The king of Byblos had the tree cut down and turned into the central column of his palace. Isis, the sister and wife of Osiris, found the column, recognized that her husband lay within, and brought it back to Egypt. Seth discovered the chest, stole it, cut Osiris' body into fourteen parts, and scattered the parts along the Nile shore. Isis searched for the parts, found them, reassembled the body of Osiris, and attempted to raise it. She failed, and her sister Nephthys tried. Nephthys failed, and their brother Thoth tried, using a special grip. The third attempt worked, and Osiris rose up to heaven.

The mysteries of Isis and Osiris included a ladder, a pillar, a tree or sprig of Acacia and a casket in their symbolism, similar to those in Freemasonry. Both went to foreign lands to share their knowledge. Both have something that evil men wish to take — the word from Hiram, the kingdom from Osiris. After a struggle both are murdered by their brothers, either biological or fraternal. Both men are hastily buried and Acacia identifies the body.
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Isis and Osiris | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#isis-and-osiris | section 10

entries.title: Exoteric and Esoteric Lessons
entries.full_summary:
## Exoteric and Esoteric Lessons

So, was Hiram Abiff developed by early Masons as an allegory for Christ, about Osiris, Noah, Adam, or a son of Cain, or about a real Hiram Abiff? Does it matter? If one takes the story at face value, as exoteric teaching, then it is a beautiful drama about virtue, integrity, and brotherhood. These are so valuable to the fraternity that the legend has great meaning, regardless of the origin, or any hidden, esoteric meaning. We don't need a metaphysical explanation or spiritual mysticism to obtain the light we are looking for. We see the underpinnings of modern ethical theories of autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice.

Esoteric teachings draw upon hidden or secret knowledge. These philosophies have been present for millennia and across all cultures and belief systems. In Masonry, the esoteric teachings from the Hiramic legend would lead us towards a deeper understanding of man's actions and motivations, having the perseverance to continue to try to raise ourselves and fellow brothers out of earthly bondage towards freedom. It is in the knowing that even when things that are truly important to us are forever lost, we can find a substitute that will allow us to continue a fulfilling life. We are reminded that there are others outside our fraternity, and some from within, who may do us harm out of jealousy or other of their uncontrolled passions. We, by understanding this legend and living through its example, can be a model to our leaders and other fellow citizens and brothers by our pure actions and goodness, despite repeated temptations. We will be better humans, husbands, fathers, sons, and Freemasons by delving deep into our histories and values that have been so richly given to us by those masons who have come before.

Hiram Abiff would have understood the sentiment from Mitch Albom, who wrote: "Sometimes when you sacrifice something precious, you're not really losing it. You're just passing it on to someone else."
source_notes: Allegories of Hiram Abiff | Exoteric and Esoteric Lessons | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Allegories_of_Hiram_Abiff.md#exoteric-and-esoteric-lessons | section 11

entries.title: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree
entries.short_summary: # The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree
entries.full_summary:
# The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree

A Lecture by W. Bro. Rob Lund, Kilwinning Lodge 565, Toronto, Ontario, Canada. March 2012.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-deeper-meaning-of-the-fc-degree | section 1
entries.work_title: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree

entries.title: Introduction: Three Degrees, Three Levels
entries.full_summary:
## Introduction: Three Degrees, Three Levels

The three degrees in Craft Masonry are designed to take the candidate through three stages of evolution. The first degree (the degree of 'birth' and the creative aspect of the GAOTU) is about moving out of the darkness of base reason, and mastery of the physical sense-being. The second degree (the degree of 'life' and the preservative aspect) is about mastery of the mind, and raising the intellect. The third degree (the degree of 'death') is about understanding the eternal nature of life, and understanding spiritual principles.

Each degree is built on the one before, so there are three levels: physical, mental, and spiritual. The Installed Master 'degree' can be seen as a further step, the degree of 'mastery' of the spiritual path.

Each degree can also be seen at three levels: physical (the ritual); mental (the moral and intellectual aspects); and spiritual (the more hidden esoteric aspect). The first level is mostly obvious and consists of all you see and hear and do in the Lodge. The second level is a little less clear, but most masons are aware of it and spend some time delving into the various moral and intellectual meanings of this level. The third level, the spiritual aspect, is the subject matter of this lecture. These Theosophic teachings, sometimes called the Secret Doctrine, can be distilled into three points: the unity of the GAOTU; the immortality of the soul; and the need to seek spiritual enlightenment in order for the soul to ascend.

The lodge and the temple, referred to throughout the degrees, are at the spiritual level a symbol of the candidate himself, and the officers represent various aspects of himself.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | Introduction: Three Degrees, Three Levels | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#introduction-three-degrees-three-levels | section 2
entries.work_title: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree

entries.title: The Ceremony of Passing
entries.full_summary:
## The Ceremony of Passing

The Second Degree is directed, mainly, to the candidate's intellectuality, so that the now purified understanding of the man of virtue may strive to attain intellectual light, or mental illumination. This is a process and experience of a mental or psychological nature, which is difficult to dramatize and make spectacular, and is therefore left somewhat to silence and the imagination. This ceremony is designed to stand in contrast with the other two, so that it should impress not by drama but by what is implied.

The Ceremony is called one of "passing", since it relates to a transitional phase of personal experience, through which every aspirant to enlightenment must pass before he can think of attaining the ultimate degree of spiritual development and mastership to which our system points. He is to take an upward step in his evolution, to enter and explore a higher level of his own being with a view to understanding and controlling it, just as he is assumed to now understand and control his physical nature, as portrayed in the first degree. On his journey from the realm of the physical senses to that of the ultimate spirit, he must pass through an intermediate region, that of the soul or mind, which is the midway between the sensible and the spiritual. Mind and Soul, for the purposes of this lecture, can be viewed as being the same thing, but should not be confused with Spirit, which is a higher level of our being.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Ceremony of Passing | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-ceremony-of-passing | section 3
entries.work_title: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree

entries.title: Examination before Passing
entries.full_summary:
## Examination before Passing

In the examination before passing, in answer to the question "how do you know yourself to be a Mason?", the candidate replies: "by the regularity of my Initiation, by repeated trials and approbations", and by a willingness to "undergo further examination when called upon".

How many times has this question been posed and answered in an almost mechanical way! How would the candidate know if his initiation has been regular? At the physical level, he may know, if he has read the Book of Constitution. However, at the mental and spiritual levels, what criteria have been used to be able to reply? What "repeated trials" has he undergone? What tests of his virtue, his courage, his purity and his faith? What "approbations" has he received, and from whom? Is he really ready and willing, in body, mind, and spirit, to undergo further, perhaps more severe, trials "when duly called upon"? Does he understand the Initiatic tradition that goes back to the dawn of history? The Master here is the Master-Principle — the conscience or inner being.

These test-questions are deep and full of meaning, which many masons are content to rush over and treat as merely routine formalities.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | Examination before Passing | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#examination-before-passing | section 4

entries.title: Opening the Lodge in the Second Degree
entries.short_summary: ## Opening the Lodge in the Second Degree
entries.full_summary:
## Opening the Lodge in the Second Degree

Opening the lodge in the Second Degree means more than just changing the working tools and the page position of the VOSL. We use the term "going up" to the Second Degree. This raising implies, and requires, a corresponding uplift of mind on the part of all present, to a higher level than what was required for the First Degree. In this Degree we are to pass, and to help the Candidate to pass, beyond the concrete things of time and space to the realm of the more abstract world of mind, of soul.

In the first degree, the body is dominant, and over-shadows the spiritual side of the Candidate's nature. In this degree, the body is dominated by the mind or soul, but as yet the spirit has not gained control over the latter. This is emphasized by the knocks, and the position of the square and compasses.

The Lodge is declared to be "opened on the Square". That simple tool takes on a philosophical value. It is composed of two arms joined at a right angle; one arm being horizontal, the other vertical. Those two arms become a metaphor of the right relationship of body and mind when we are engaged in the mystical labour of the Second Degree: the bodily energies (the horizontal arm) must now lie down and be passive, while the higher faculties of mind and soul (the upright arm) should become active and aspire upwards. Every one in the Lodge is required to "prove himself" a Mason of this Degree — to demonstrate, by a ceremonial gesture, that his outward and inward energies stand in the right relationship as symbolized by the arms of the square. Only when all those present prove themselves united in this condition can the Lodge truly be opened on the Square.

In the First Degree, all the knocks are evenly spaced. In the Fellowcraft Degree, two are joined. This indicates that the soul and body are now working in union although, as yet, the purely spiritual faculties have little influence.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | Opening the Lodge in the Second Degree | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#opening-the-lodge-in-the-second-degree | section 5

entries.title: Perambulation: The Soul's Forward Movement
entries.full_summary:
## Perambulation: The Soul's Forward Movement

The perambulations around the Lodge in the First Degree symbolized the Candidate's wandering in the darkness before he found the path to Light. In this degree, they signify that the human soul should continue searching for enlightenment and spiritual growth.

The candidate should think of these mystical journeys around the Lodge as symbolic of his soul's continued forward movement to his desired spiritual goal. The Deacon who guides him is symbolic of his own unerring enlightened conscience, his inner guide. The salutes he makes to the Master during his progress are recognition of higher spiritual powers within himself. The examinations he has to undergo by the Wardens (the psyche and the intellect) should be understood as the testing of his qualifications to spiritual advancement, which every soul experiences on its upward path.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | Perambulation: The Soul's Forward Movement | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#perambulation-the-soul-s-forward-movement | section 6

entries.title: The Winding Staircase: Ascent of the Mind
entries.short_summary: ## The Winding Staircase: Ascent of the Mind
entries.full_summary:
## The Winding Staircase: Ascent of the Mind

The Mind or Soul (represented by the Senior Warden) tells the Divine Spirit (represented by the Worshipful Master) that the candidate is ready to be passed, and the Soul tells the intelligence (the link between spirit and mind, represented by the Senior Deacon) to instruct the Candidate how to advance.

The perambulations were made on the level floor of the Lodge, which the Candidate keeps on "squaring", visiting each of its four sides in turn. But now his forward motion on the level ceases, and he is directed to mount, spirally, by a certain number of winding steps. Motion now becomes circular, and he advances not merely forward, but up, and in, towards the centre. This change of motion, this spiral ascent, is the most significant symbolism in this degree: the time has come when the Candidate must leave the level of the sense-world and rise to the supra-sensual. At the summit of this stairway is the middle chamber of the temple — the candidate is approaching his centre.

From the moment of ascending the winding staircase, the Candidate is mentally leaving the outer world more and more behind him, and rising up into an inner invisible world. He is making what has been called "the ascent of the mind to the Source of Light", and it is to these new regions, and the learning of their secrets and mysteries, that his labours as a Fellow Craft should be devoted.

This ascent is made towards the East (the source of Light) by a journey of five steps. Why five? Man's nature is resolvable into a series or spectrum of seven distinct principles (corresponding with the seven officers forming a Lodge), but of these seven, the two lowest are left out in this Degree: the senses and the carnal reason, both physical, are left behind and transcended, whilst the five higher, psychic and spiritual, faculties alone come into function. The five-pointed star is a geometrical symbol of man's five higher principles.

Learning to dissociate the five higher principles from the two lower ones is one of the chief lessons of this Degree. In coming to any true knowledge of ourselves, we must begin by discriminating between what belongs to the sense-world and the supra-sensual world respectively — to distinguish between things transient and things enduring. It is the work of the Second Degree to train the mind, and higher principles, to function consciously apart from the senses.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Winding Staircase: Ascent of the Mind | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-winding-staircase-ascent-of-the-mind | section 7

entries.title: The Obligation: Head and Heart
entries.full_summary:
## The Obligation: Head and Heart

When the candidate takes his obligation, there are five squares present: the position in which the candidate takes his obligation makes three squares, and the instrument which supports his arm, and the one on the VOSL, produce two more — five in all, corresponding to the five senses.

The distinction between the things of the head and those of the "heart" accounts for the difference in the posture assumed by the Candidate when taking his Obligation. The right side and limbs of the body are associated with the head, and the left with the "heart". In the First Degree certain energies are required to be active and others passive; in the Second Degree their relationship must be reversed. This is why first the right and then the left side of the body is divested in the successive Degrees: the right side is associated with active effort, and the left with passive receptivity.

In this degree, the head is enlightened by the heart so that reason and intuition function in unity, and cannot act separately, as either of them individually could prove a treacherous and misleading faculty — wrong-headedness is far more common than evil-heartedness, and responsible for far more mischief and suffering.

The Candidate for self-knowledge has to train himself to understand and discipline both his head and his heart; to balance activity with contemplation; to labour zealously at practising virtue, but also to "study to be quiet"; to watch for and examine perceptions, enthusiasms and passionate urges that well up from within him; above all to listen for the "still small voice" that may be heard speaking in his heart when passion and the senses subside.

In the First Degree, the penalty is related to the head: infidelity, in the form of abuse of speech, cuts off the inner voice. In the Second Degree, the penalty relates to the "heart", which, if unfaithful to the purpose of this degree, may become "uprooted", as in the expression "that man has no heart". It is possible to sin psychically as well as physically, and the prescribed penalties have a concealed link to the offences involved. The Ritual goes on to say that "you are now permitted to extend your researches into the more hidden paths of nature and science" — until one possesses a high degree of personal purity, virtue and understanding, such research is not "permitted".

The altered relationship of the Square and Compasses implies that the candidate's latent spiritual principle (the compasses) is at last beginning to emerge from dormancy into activity and personal consciousness, whilst his subordinate personality and physical sense-being (the square) recedes correspondingly into the background.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Obligation: Head and Heart | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-obligation-head-and-heart | section 8
entries.work_title: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree

entries.title: The Secrets and the Ear of Corn
entries.short_summary: ## The Secrets and the Ear of Corn
entries.full_summary:
## The Secrets and the Ear of Corn

"The secrets of each Degree are to be kept separate and distinct from those in the former," says the Ritual. The secret of the First Degree had to do with the head — with the practical every-day intelligence and the performance of active duties. Those of the Second Degree are secrets of the "heart" or soul; of the intuitional and affectional side of our nature, which is subjective and passive.

The word of this degree is a Hebrew word signifying "sprouting forth". It is expressive of the Candidate himself at this time: as the result of his work in the First Degree and of the trials and approbations he has undergone, new life has germinated within him. The ear of corn is a symbol of the Candidate's own soul growth, nourished by the fall upon it of the Living Water of Enlightenment. He is already a changed man and beginning to "sprout forth" spiritually.

In the Egyptian Rituals, the Candidate, holding an ear of corn fertilized by the sacred water of the Nile, declared "I am a germ of eternity!", and at his death grains of corn were buried with him as emblems of immortality. In the Eleusinian Mysteries, one of the most advanced and secret initiation rites was that in which an ear of corn was presented to the Candidate when the "mysteries of Ceres" associated with it were revealed to him.

At the consecration of every Masonic Lodge, grains of corn are scattered to the four quarters of space; the Second Degree tracing board displays growing corn, whilst the "full corn in the ear" is exhibited in gold embroidery on the full dress collars of Grand Lodge officers — an emblem that what once was sown in them as bare grain has at last ripened to full and prolific fruit. It is a symbol meant to be personally used: an idea to be taken into private meditation and mentally dwelt upon until it ceases to be a symbol.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Secrets and the Ear of Corn | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-secrets-and-the-ear-of-corn | section 9

entries.title: The Apron in the Second Degree
entries.short_summary: ## The Apron in the Second Degree
entries.full_summary:
## The Apron in the Second Degree

The triangular flap of the apron represents the spirit, while the rectangular part represents the physical. In some Masonic jurisdictions, Entered Apprentice masons wear the flap turned. The flap is turned down in the second degree to indicate that the spiritual is now overlaying the physical. The two rosettes represent the physical and the mental.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Apron in the Second Degree | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-apron-in-the-second-degree | section 10

entries.title: The Tracing Board: Wages and the Middle Chamber
entries.short_summary: ## The Tracing Board: Wages and the Middle Chamber
entries.full_summary:
## The Tracing Board: Wages and the Middle Chamber

The Senior Warden's lecture around the second degree Tracing Board includes an allegory of the wages paid at the temple. The Entered Apprentices, representing those who are not yet very spiritually evolved, obtained merely simple maintenance (corn, wine, and oil), whereas the Fellow Crafts were paid their wages in specie, which, according to American ritual, they could only receive in the Middle Chamber. Their wages were of a spiritual nature, suitable to their more evolved spirituality, and they received them in the middle chamber, which is an allegory for the secret chamber of the Heart (the centre), wherein resides the Divine Spark. In Ontario ritual this is implied: a password was required by the Fellowcraft to ascend the winding staircase which led to the middle chamber of the temple.

The winding staircase spirals round a central column, so that when the candidates reached the top, after three, five, seven, or more steps, they had advanced neither to the East nor to the West, but were still revolving around the centre.

The three who rule a Lodge represent the Body, Soul, and Spirit, which together constitute Man. The five who form a Lodge are the five senses of the physical man. To find our own Centre (our real self) involves a turning inwards of our previously externalized faculties of sense and thought, until the Centre is found. This task is represented by our ceremonial perambulations and by the path of the winding staircase, up which the aspirant must ascend — asking, seeking, knocking all the way, being subjected from time to time to tests of his progress. The innermost sanctuary is closely guarded: nothing unclean can enter that holy place. In the biblical description of the symbolic Temple, in the forecourt stood the great laver of water for the cleansing of pollutions, and the altar of fire for the sacrificial burning up of impurities. The password of the degree prevents any unqualified person from ascending the winding staircase — a test of merit for the whole nature.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Tracing Board: Wages and the Middle Chamber | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-tracing-board-wages-and-the-middle-chamber | section 11

entries.title: The Letter G and the Five-Pointed Star
entries.short_summary: ## The Letter G and the Five-Pointed Star
entries.full_summary:
## The Letter G and the Five-Pointed Star

The "G" had its equivalent in the Egyptian Mysteries in the solar symbol of Ra, the spiritual Sun. In the great temple of the Greek Mysteries at Delphi, it was represented by the fifth letter of the Greek alphabet (Eta); five being a numerical symbol of man in the Pythagorean system, as evidenced by his five senses. Hence the five-pointed star is also a symbol of man. In the rituals described in the Book of the Dead, the candidate is described as a "keeper of five"; Operative fellow-craft Masons worked in groups of five, and a fellow-craft Lodge today consists of five brethren ("five keep a lodge").

Centrally, suspended from the ceiling in the centre of each Lodge, is exhibited this striking symbol. It is the emblem of the Divine Presence at the spiritual centre of the individual Mason. The fact that it is in the Centre reminds us of that hidden centre in every man, where resides the Divine Spark.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | The Letter G and the Five-Pointed Star | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#the-letter-g-and-the-five-pointed-star | section 12
entries.work_title: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree
entries.source_heading: The Letter G and the Five-Pointed Star

entries.title: Closing and Summary
entries.full_summary:
## Closing and Summary

In the closing of this degree, it is noteworthy that it is the Junior Warden (representing the Body) who plays the most important part — appropriate, as we have been dealing throughout with the body and its senses. At the centre of ourselves is the "All Seeing Eye", so that we ourselves constitute our own Judgment Book, wherein each of us inscribes his own history and determines his own destiny.

To summarize: in the first degree, the sense-nature must be brought into subjugation and the practice of virtue acquired before the mind can be educated; in the second degree, the mind, in turn, must be educated, disciplined, and controlled, before truths that transcend the mind can be perceived. The candidate must understand that he has to rise up from the base physical nature, and prepare his mind and intellect for further enlightenment, and seek that enlightenment from the centre of his being.
source_notes: The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree | Closing and Summary | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\Deeper meaning of FC Degree.md#closing-and-summary | section 13
